state.prose:
As of 2026-03-10, recent behavioral observations (overall confidence: high):

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 23:57 (about 0.7h later than typical 23:17); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]
  • Sleep has averaged 7.0h (0.4h shorter than typical 7.4h); drifting over the last 3d [mild, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 86% vs personal baseline (56min vs typical 30min); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]
  • Total screen time elevated (293min/day vs typical 260min, +13%); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.

coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Late-night screen use spiked to ~56 min yesterday (vs. ~30 min baseline), coinciding with sleep onset drifting ~40 min later than typical. These two markers cohere as a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern: evening screen engagement is likely delaying the transition to sleep. The mild but emerging 3-day shortfall in sleep duration (~0.4h/night) is a downstream consequence of later onset without a compensating later wake time.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck phone-mediated sleep delay; extended here to include cumulative duration shortfall
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

response.salience_reasoning: All four deviations are mild and mostly acute (1-day), but the sleep-duration shortfall has been drifting for 3 days, giving it slightly more weight than the single-day screen spikes. The pre-existing coherent pattern (phone-mediated-sleep-delay) is confirmed and modestly extended by the total screen time elevation. The most actionable leverage point on the calendar is tonight's 'optional work session' (21:00–22:30): it runs close to the user's typical sleep onset (~23:17) and, if it bleeds into screen use afterward, directly feeds the delay pattern. A light wind-down buffer after that block is the minimal, low-burden intervention. No other calendar events this week appear to be contributing factors.

suggestions.change: Cap the 'optional work session' at 21:45 and add a 15-min Wind-down buffer block (21:45–22:00) as a soft reminder to close screens before the session ends
suggestions.rationale: The optional work session ends at 22:30, leaving under an hour before the user's baseline sleep onset (~23:17). Given that late-night screen use spiked yesterday and sleep onset drifted later, ending active screen work earlier and inserting a brief transition block reduces the risk of the session spilling into the delay window.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Screens off' reminder block at 22:30
suggestions.rationale: A hard-stop marker at 22:30 gives a ~45-min wind-down runway before the user's typical sleep onset, directly countering the late-night screen pattern observed yesterday. This is a low-burden nudge consistent with the user's preference for minimal-friction changes.

Sleep:

  • Sleep has averaged 6.7h (0.7h shorter than typical 7.4h); drifting over the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]

  • Total screen time elevated (319min/day vs typical 259min, +23%); intermittently (2d of 3) [moderate, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset has drifted ~1.2h later over the past 3 days, co-occurring with a 158% spike in late-night screen use (78 min vs. 30 min baseline). The screen activity is the most plausible proximate driver of the delayed onset, consistent with light/arousal-mediated sleep delay. This confirms the prior hypothesis from the deterministic rules, with the additional note that total screen time is also elevated (+23%), suggesting the pattern extends beyond just the late-night window.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck phone-mediated sleep delay
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep regularity index has dropped (75 vs. 84 baseline) alongside both a later sleep onset and shortened sleep duration (6.7h vs. 7.4h baseline). The combination of irregular timing and reduced duration creates compounding sleep debt. This extends the prior circadian-instability hypothesis by adding the duration shortfall as a third co-occurring marker, suggesting the instability is not just a timing shift but is also cutting into total sleep.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Walker circadian instability + sleep debt; Lipson/Lewy sleep regularity
coherent_patterns.name: circadian-instability-with-sleep-debt

questions_for_user: Is the late-night screen use typically work/study related, or more passive browsing/entertainment? This would help decide whether earlier study scheduling or a general screen curfew is the better lever.
response.salience_reasoning: The most actionable cluster this week is the phone-mediated-sleep-delay / circadian-instability-with-sleep-debt compound: three sleep markers and two screen markers are all moving in the same direction over the same 3-day window, with high coverage confidence. The late-night screen use is the highest-magnitude single deviation (+158%) and is the most tractable lever — reducing it is likely to pull sleep onset earlier, which in turn should stabilize the regularity index and recover some duration. The calendar already has an 'optional work session' ending at 22:30 on 2026-03-11, which is a direct structural risk factor for continued late-night screen use on a night when sleep onset is already drifting past midnight. That event is marked flexible, making it the clearest near-term intervention point. Total screen time elevation is a secondary concern — it reinforces the pattern but is less directly addressable via calendar than the late-night window. No feedback history exists, so all suggestions are fresh.

suggestions.change: End or convert the 'optional work session' (currently 21:00–22:30) to a hard stop at 21:30, freeing 21:30–22:30 as an unplugged wind-down block
suggestions.rationale: The optional work session runs until 22:30, which directly overlaps with the late-night screen window driving delayed sleep onset. Capping active screen work at 21:30 and protecting the following hour as screen-light reduces the primary driver of the sleep delay pattern. The event is already marked flexible, so this is a low-disruption change.

suggestions.change: Add a recurring 'Wind-down / no screens' block at 22:30 for the next 5 weekdays
suggestions.rationale: A consistent pre-sleep anchor at 22:30 creates a daily regularity cue that directly targets the circadian-instability-with-sleep-debt pattern. Keeping the same wind-down start each night is one of the lowest-burden ways to nudge sleep onset earlier and improve the regularity index without requiring any daytime schedule changes.

suggestions.change: Move the flexible 'study block' (currently 15:00–17:00) earlier to 13:00–15:00 on days where morning schedule permits, to reduce evening workload pressure that may be driving late-night catch-up sessions
suggestions.rationale: Late-night screen use is intermittent (2 of 3 days), suggesting it may be triggered by unfinished daytime work rather than pure habit. Shifting the study block earlier reduces the likelihood that work spills into the post-22:00 window, complementing the wind-down block suggestion.

state.prose:

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 01:04 (about 1.8h later than typical 23:18); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • Sleep has averaged 6.3h (1.1h shorter than typical 7.4h); sustained across the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]
  • Sleep schedule has been less consistent (SRI 70 vs typical 84); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 260% vs personal baseline (104min vs typical 29min); sustained across the last 3d [pronounced, coverage: high]
  • Total screen time elevated (343min/day vs typical 262min, +31%); sustained across the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]
  • Attention pattern more fragmented than baseline (1.7 vs 1.2 switches/active-min); sustained across the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)
  • Sleep schedule consistency has dropped; circadian rhythm is less stable than typical. Anchoring morning wake time and fixed daily events can help.
    (signals: sleep_regularity_index, sleep_onset_hour)
  • Attention is more fragmented while sleep is shorter — attention costs typically rise when sleep debt accumulates. Worth protecting sleep opportunity over discretionary evening commitments.
    (signals: app_switching_rate, sleep_duration_hours)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset has shifted ~1.8h later (to ~01:04) alongside a 260% spike in late-night screen use (104 min vs. 29 min baseline). The screen activity is the most plausible proximate driver of the delayed onset — light and cognitive arousal from devices push sleep timing later. This pattern has been sustained for 3 days, making it the highest-priority lever.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck (2016) phone-mediated sleep delay
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep regularity index has dropped from 84 to 70 (a pronounced shift) while sleep onset is consistently late. Together these indicate the sleep schedule has lost its anchor — not just shifted, but become less predictable day-to-day. Elevated total screen time (+31%) likely extends wakefulness variably each night, compounding the irregularity.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Walker (2017) circadian regularity; Monk et al. SRM social-rhythm disruption
coherent_patterns.name: circadian-instability

coherent_patterns.interpretation: App-switching rate is up 44% above baseline while sleep duration is down ~1.1h/night — a combination where reduced sleep opportunity and elevated screen engagement reinforce each other. Higher switching may reflect difficulty sustaining focus, which in turn drives more screen browsing, which delays sleep further. This forms a self-reinforcing loop across all three screen markers and both sleep markers.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Saeb et al. fragmented-attention / sleep-debt cognitive strain
coherent_patterns.name: fragmented-attention-with-sleep-loss

questions_for_user: The late-night screen time has tripled over the past 3 days — is there a specific reason for that (e.g., a deadline, a show, or social catch-up)? Knowing whether it's time-limited would help calibrate how much to adjust the coming week.
Is the 21:00–22:30 optional work session something you'd be comfortable ending earlier, or does it feel necessary given current workload?
response.salience_reasoning: All six deviations are high-coverage, sustained over 3 days, and cluster tightly into three overlapping patterns that share a common mechanism: late-night screen use is delaying sleep onset, shortening sleep duration, destabilizing the sleep schedule, and co-occurring with fragmented daytime attention. The single most actionable leverage point is the evening — specifically the 'optional work session' on today's calendar (21:00–22:30), which runs directly into the window where late-night screen use is spiking. Ending or replacing that block with a wind-down routine would address phone-mediated-sleep-delay and circadian-instability simultaneously. A fixed morning anchor would help stabilize the SRI. The fragmented-attention pattern is downstream of the sleep issues and does not need a separate calendar intervention beyond protecting sleep opportunity.

suggestions.change: Replace or end the 'optional work session' (21:00–22:30) by 21:30 and add a Wind-down block from 21:30–22:30 — screen-free or low-stimulation activity only.
suggestions.rationale: Late-night screen use is running 260% above baseline and is the most direct driver of the delayed sleep onset. The optional work session occupies exactly the window where screen activity is spiking. Capping it at 21:30 and protecting the remainder as wind-down time removes the primary opportunity for late-night screen use without touching any fixed commitments.

suggestions.change: Add a fixed 'Lights-out target' reminder block at 23:00 for the next 5 nights.
suggestions.rationale: Sleep onset has drifted to ~01:04 and the regularity index has dropped to 70. A consistent, earlier target sleep time — even as a soft reminder — provides a daily anchor that can help pull the schedule back toward baseline and reduce night-to-night variability.

suggestions.change: Add a fixed 'Morning anchor' wake block at 07:30 each day this week — keep it consistent even on days with no early commitments.
suggestions.rationale: A stable wake time is one of the most reliable ways to re-anchor a drifting sleep schedule. With SRI at 70 (vs. 84 baseline), holding the wake time constant creates a forward pressure on sleep onset timing regardless of how late the previous night ran.

Mobility & location routine:
  • Location routine more restricted than baseline (entropy 1.48 vs 1.87); drifting over the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]
  • More time at top-3 frequent places (70% vs typical 58%); (yesterday only) [mild, coverage: high]

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Both mobility markers are moving in the same direction: location entropy has dropped moderately over the past 3 days (1.48 vs baseline 1.87) while time concentrated at the top-3 familiar places has risen (70% vs baseline 58%). The 3-day drift in entropy gives this pattern more weight than the single-day revisit spike alone. Together they indicate a narrowing of movement range and reduced engagement with varied locations — consistent with a behavioral-withdrawal pattern.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Saeb et al. mobility-entropy depression correlate; behavioral withdrawal syndrome
coherent_patterns.name: behavioral-withdrawal

questions_for_user: Is the reduced movement range intentional this week (e.g., a deadline crunch keeping you close to home/campus)? That would help calibrate whether these nudges are useful or unnecessary.
response.salience_reasoning: There are only two deviations, and they cohere into a single pattern: the user's physical range has been contracting for 3 days, with time increasingly concentrated at familiar spots. The calendar for today is already dense (class, project meeting, study block, evening deadline block), so the most useful nudge is a low-burden prompt to break the location routine — not by adding heavy commitments, but by suggesting a brief out-of-routine errand or walk that slots naturally around existing fixed events. No sleep, social, or attention markers are implicated, so suggestions should stay narrow. Confidence is high and the pattern has a 3-day trajectory, making a gentle proactive nudge appropriate.

suggestions.change: Add a short 'Change of scenery' walk or errand block (15–20 min) between the project meeting and the afternoon study block
suggestions.rationale: A brief excursion to a less-frequented location during the natural gap in the schedule (11:15–15:00) is a low-effort way to interrupt the narrowing location routine without touching any fixed events or the evening deadline block.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Get out of usual spot' reminder block on one afternoon later this week (e.g., a café study session or campus library instead of the usual location)
suggestions.rationale: The 3-day drift in mobility entropy suggests the pattern is not a one-off; a second scheduled nudge mid-week reinforces variety before the pattern deepens further.

response.salience_reasoning: All four deviations cohere into a single behavioral-withdrawal pattern, making this the dominant signal for calendar planning this week. The mobility and location markers are pronounced and have been drifting for 3 days, which gives them priority. The social-rhythm irregularity is moderate and intermittent but reinforces the picture — routine anchor events are slipping in timing. The screen-time rise is mild on its own but fits as a behavioral complement (more time at home → more passive screen use). Because overall confidence is high and the pattern is multi-signal, concrete but low-burden suggestions are warranted. The user's calendar shows a late evening 'deadline work block' (20:00–22:00) tonight, which is a natural place to introduce a brief wind-down anchor afterward to help re-regularize the social rhythm. Adding a short out-of-home or change-of-scene prompt mid-week would gently counter the location narrowing without disrupting fixed academic events.

suggestions.change: Add a 15-min 'Step outside / change of scene' prompt block on a mid-week morning or lunch slot (e.g., 12:30–12:45) on a day with no back-to-back fixed commitments

A brief, low-effort prompt to leave the top-3 familiar locations — even a short walk — directly targets the location-narrowing component of the behavioral-withdrawal pattern without requiring significant schedule restructuring.

suggestions.change: Add a 'Wind-down / screens off' block at 22:00 on evenings with flexible late work blocks

Placing a hard wind-down anchor immediately after the work block helps re-regularize the social rhythm (SRM is down) and caps the screen-time drift before it compounds, consistent with the user's preference to protect sleep.

suggestions.change: Add a recurring 'Consistent wake anchor' reminder at the same time each morning for the next 3 days (e.g., 08:00) to help re-stabilize daily routine timing
suggestions.rationale: The social rhythm metric has been intermittently irregular (2 of 3 recent days), suggesting anchor-event timing is drifting. A lightweight morning anchor — even just a phone reminder — can help re-synchronize the daily rhythm without adding burden, consistent with Monk et al. SRM stabilization principles.

The prior deterministic pattern (behavioral-withdrawal) is confirmed and extended: total_screen_min fits the picture as a co-occurring marker, likely filling time that would otherwise involve varied locations or structured social anchors. The most actionable calendar lever is daily-routine regularity (SRM is the most directly schedulable signal), followed by creating a natural reason to leave familiar locations. The evening 'deadline work block' (20:00–22:00) is flexible and sits at a time where a brief out-of-home or structured social anchor could be inserted without disrupting fixed commitments. Screen time elevation is mild and secondary; no standalone action is warranted for it beyond what routine-anchoring would naturally address.

The mobility-entropy dip is mild, acute (1 day only), and stands alone without a co-occurring marker (e.g., no reciprocity or social-contact drop is reported), so it does not yet form a coherent pattern and warrants only a light question rather than a calendar change. On the calendar side, tonight's 'optional work session' runs until 22:30 — this is a direct leverage point, since it sits right in the late-night screen window and could be nudging screen use later. Shortening or capping that block is the most targeted, low-burden intervention available.

suggestions.change: End the 'optional work session' by 21:45 instead of 22:30, and add a short Wind-down block from 21:45–22:15 (no work screens)
suggestions.rationale: The optional work session currently runs to 22:30, which overlaps with the late-night screen window where usage has been elevated for 3 days. Trimming it by ~45 minutes and replacing the tail with a screen-free wind-down creates a natural cutoff before the drift window, giving sleep onset a better chance of returning toward the typical 23:07.

state.prose:
As of 2026-03-11, recent behavioral observations (overall confidence: high):

Sleep:
  • Sleep onset has been around 00:11 (about 1.1h later than typical 23:07); intermittently (2d of 3) [moderate, coverage: high]

Screen & attention:
  • Late-night screen use up 121% vs personal baseline (76min vs typical 35min); drifting over the last 3d [moderate, coverage: high]

Mobility & location routine:
  • Location routine more restricted than baseline (entropy 1.36 vs 1.78); intermittently (2d of 3) [moderate, coverage: high]
  • More time at top-3 frequent places (71% vs typical 61%); intermittently (2d of 3) [mild, coverage: high]

Social rhythm & communication:
  • Daily routine timing less regular (SRM 0.66 vs typical 0.80); drifting over the last 3d [mild, coverage: high]

Coherent patterns worth noting:
  • Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
    (signals: sleep_onset_hour, late_night_screen_min)
  • Location routine has narrowed and time concentrated at frequent places — a behavioral-withdrawal pattern often associated with lower mood and reduced third-place engagement.
    (signals: mobility_entropy, location_revisit_ratio, social_rhythm_metric)

The LLM scheduler should decide which of the above are worth acting on through calendar adjustments, based on the user's preferences, upcoming commitments, and the coherence of the pattern — not on single-day magnitudes.

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset is drifting later alongside increased late-night screen use — a phone-mediated sleep-delay pattern. Evening calendar density and wind-down timing are plausible leverage points.
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Location routine has narrowed and time concentrated at frequent places — a behavioral-withdrawal pattern often associated with lower mood and reduced third-place engagement.
coherent_patterns.name: behavioral-withdrawal

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Sleep onset has shifted ~1.1h later than baseline, co-occurring with a 121% rise in late-night screen use over the same 3-day window. The drift trajectory on screen use and the intermittent-but-repeating late sleep onset are consistent with screen engagement delaying sleep timing. This confirms and extends the prior hypothesis: the pattern is now showing a clear drift direction rather than a one-off spike.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Exelmans & Van den Bulck — late-night smartphone use and sleep onset delay
coherent_patterns.name: phone-mediated-sleep-delay

coherent_patterns.interpretation: Mobility entropy is down (fewer distinct locations visited) and time at the top-3 familiar places is up, both intermittently over 3 days. These two markers move in the same direction and reinforce each other: the user's physical range has narrowed and routine has concentrated at habitual spots. The prior hypothesis is confirmed; no contradiction found.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Saeb et al. mobility-entropy depression correlate
coherent_patterns.name: behavioral-withdrawal

coherent_patterns.interpretation: The social rhythm metric has drifted down over 3 days, meaning the timing of daily anchor events (meals, sleep, social contact) is becoming less regular. This co-occurs with the shifting sleep onset, suggesting the two are mutually reinforcing: irregular anchor timing makes it harder to maintain a consistent sleep schedule, and a drifting sleep schedule destabilizes other daily rhythms. This extends the phone-mediated-sleep-delay pattern into a broader circadian/social-rhythm dimension.
coherent_patterns.literature_basis: Monk et al. Social Rhythm Metric — irregular anchor-event timing and mood instability
coherent_patterns.name: social-rhythm-instability

response.salience_reasoning: Two multi-marker patterns dominate this week. The most actionable is the phone-mediated sleep delay: late-night screen use is drifting upward and sleep onset is following it later, and tonight's calendar already has an 'optional work session' running until 22:30 — which directly feeds into the pattern. A wind-down block replacing or capping that session is a low-burden, high-leverage intervention. The behavioral-withdrawal pattern (narrowed mobility, concentration at familiar places) is real but intermittent; a single low-friction prompt to schedule one out-of-routine activity is appropriate without over-engineering. The social rhythm drift is a supporting signal that reinforces the case for anchoring the evening routine at a consistent time. No single-marker orphan deviations remain — all five markers are accounted for across the three patterns.

suggestions.change: Replace or cap the 'optional work session' (currently 21:00–22:30) with a hard stop at 21:45, and add a Wind-down block from 21:45–22:30 (no screens, light activity only)
suggestions.rationale: The optional work session runs until 22:30, which is precisely the window where late-night screen use has been elevated. Ending work earlier and inserting a screen-free wind-down block removes the primary driver of delayed sleep onset without cancelling the session entirely — a low-burden change that directly targets the drift.

suggestions.change: Add a consistent 'Morning anchor' block at 08:00 on days without an early fixed commitment — a brief, repeatable routine (e.g., 15-min walk or breakfast away from desk)
suggestions.rationale: Social rhythm regularity has been drifting. Anchoring the start of the day at a consistent time on open mornings helps stabilize daily rhythm timing, which in turn supports more consistent sleep onset in the evening.

suggestions.change: Add one 'Out-of-routine activity' block (e.g., a walk to a new location, a café, or a brief errand outside habitual places) for ~45 minutes sometime mid-week
suggestions.rationale: Mobility has been concentrating at the top-3 familiar locations. A single scheduled outing to a less-frequent place is a minimal nudge that gently counters the narrowing-range pattern without requiring a large commitment.

questions_for_user: The last 3 evenings show a lot more screen time after 21:00 — is there something specific driving that (e.g., a project deadline, a show, or just habit)? Knowing this would help tailor the wind-down suggestion.
Your location routine has narrowed noticeably this week — is that intentional (e.g., staying in to focus) or something you'd like to change?
response.salience_reasoning: Two multi-signal patterns are both sustained across 3 days and high-confidence, making them the clear priorities. The phone-mediated-sleep-delay pattern is the most actionable via calendar: the 'optional work session' (21:00–22:30) sits directly in the window where late-night screen use is elevated and is a flexible event — moving or capping it is a low-burden lever. The behavioral-withdrawal pattern is harder to address with a single calendar block, but adding a brief out-of-home anchor (e.g., a walk or errand) on a weekday afternoon could gently counteract the narrowing location routine and help re-regularize daily timing. The social rhythm irregularity (SRM drop) suggests that consistent anchor times — especially a fixed wind-down start — could help stabilize the daily schedule. No single-marker deviations exist outside these two patterns, so all five deviations are accounted for.

suggestions.change: Replace or cap the 'optional work session' (21:00–22:30) with a hard stop at 21:30, and add a Wind-down block from 21:30–22:30
suggestions.rationale: Late-night screen use is running ~60 min over baseline and sleep onset has shifted 1.5h later over 3 consecutive days. The optional work session ending at 22:30 is a flexible event that sits squarely in the elevated screen-use window. Capping work at 21:30 and replacing the tail with a wind-down block creates a structural prompt to reduce screen engagement before the target sleep window.

suggestions.change: Add a 30-minute 'Out-of-home break' block on a weekday afternoon (e.g., 14:30–15:00)
suggestions.rationale: Mobility entropy is down 34% and time at familiar locations is up to 76% over 3 days. A short, low-effort out-of-home slot (walk, coffee run, errand) adds a new location visit without requiring significant schedule disruption, gently nudging location variety back toward baseline.

suggestions.change: Add a fixed 'Anchor check-in' reminder at 22:45 each evening this week
suggestions.rationale: The social rhythm metric has dropped to 0.63 vs. a typical 0.80, indicating daily timing has become less regular. A lightweight nightly anchor — even just a consistent reminder to begin winding down — can help re-regularize the daily schedule without adding burden.
